_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Jefferson County, TN. Formed 1792 from Greene and Hawkins counties — predates Tennessee statehood. Seat: Dandridge. The courthouse survived the Civil War but Douglas Dam (TVA, 1943) came close to flooding the town — Dandridge is one of the few towns saved from TVA flooding by direct congressional intervention. Records from 1792.

current_courthouse.location: Dandridge, TN — Main Street. One of the oldest courthouses in Tennessee, nearly lost to TVA flooding.

previous_courthouses.fate: Early log and frame courthouses. Records transferred to the current building at completion.

record_losses.cause: None — no major fire or war destruction. Dandridge narrowly avoided TVA flooding.
record_losses.description: Jefferson County's 1845 courthouse survived the Civil War and was nearly lost to TVA's Douglas Lake in 1943. The original dam plans would have flooded Dandridge, but congressional intervention raised the town's levee and saved both the town and its records. The courthouse and its records are intact from the county's 1792 formation. Pre-statehood records (1792-1796) include NC Territorial period documents.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} lived in Jefferson County after 1792, the records survive. The near-loss to TVA flooding is itself a story — Dandridge was saved by political intervention that most displaced valley communities did not receive.
record_losses.source: Tennessee State Library and Archives. Guide to County Records: Jefferson County.

deeds.completeness: Excellent — continuous from formation, predating statehood
deeds.repository: Jefferson County Register of Deeds, Dandridge; TSLA microfilm

wills_estates.gaps: Earliest loose papers may be fragmentary.

court_minutes.completeness: Good — continuous with Civil War disruption

substitute_sources.description: Douglas Dam displaced valley families from French Broad bottomland. TVA land acquisition records document every condemned tract. The dam was built in twelve months (wartime emergency), leaving families minimal time to contest or prepare.
substitute_sources.repository: Tennessee Valley Authority Technical Library; National Archives Southeast Region
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Critical for tracking families displaced by the dam. Names, tract descriptions, compensation amounts, and in some cases relocation destinations.
substitute_sources.source: Tennessee Valley Authority. Douglas Dam and Reservoir: Final Environmental Impact Statement. Knoxville: TVA, 1972.

substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for tracking families. Manufacturing schedules (1850-1880) document zinc mining operations.
substitute_sources.source: NARA. Federal Census Schedules, Jefferson County, TN.

substitute_sources.description: Carson-Newman College (Baptist, founded 1851 as Mossy Creek Baptist Seminary) in Jefferson City maintained student records, faculty records, and institutional correspondence that name local families.
substitute_sources.repository: Carson-Newman University Archives, Jefferson City, TN
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Student enrollment records track families who invested in education. Faculty and staff records document institutional employees. Church-related correspondence names community members.

substitute_sources.description: Zinc mining operations at New Market and the Mossy Creek area maintained employment and production records. Where these survive, they document miners and smelter workers.
substitute_sources.repository: Fragmentary — some at TSLA, some at USGS archives. Corporate records from defunct mining companies may be in private hands.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Mine employment records can document workers who left no other county records. Occupational hazard documentation (injuries, deaths) names individuals.
substitute_sources.source: United States Geological Survey. Mineral Resources of Tennessee: Zinc and Lead. USGS, various years.

narrative_hooks: Jefferson County's 1845 courthouse nearly went underwater when TVA built Douglas Dam — but Congress saved Dandridge, and {ancestor_name}'s records survived in the same building where they were filed.
The French Broad bottomland that {ancestor_name} farmed is under Douglas Lake now — but the deed that described the land and the will that passed it down are still in the courthouse on the hill.

Jefferson County records go back to 1792 — four years before Tennessee became a state. {ancestor_name}'s earliest transactions were conducted under North Carolina's authority in a territory that would soon become a new state.

If {ancestor_name} worked the zinc mines, the mining company records and the federal mineral surveys may document what the courthouse could not — employment, wages, and the occupational hazards of underground work.
source_references: Tennessee State Library and Archives. Guide to County Records: Jefferson County.
Tennessee Valley Authority. Douglas Dam and Reservoir: Final Environmental Impact Statement. Knoxville: TVA, 1972.
United States Geological Survey. Mineral Resources of Tennessee: Zinc and Lead. USGS, various years.
